content.explanation:
React.memo is a higher-order component that wraps a functional component and prevents it from re-rendering if its props have not changed. By default, React re-renders a child component whenever its parent re-renders, even if the child's props are identical. React.memo adds a shallow comparison of the previous and next props - if all props are the same by reference, the render is skipped.

Shallow comparison means React checks each prop with Object.is (essentially ===). Primitives (strings, numbers, booleans) are compared by value, so they work naturally. But objects, arrays, and functions are compared by reference. If a parent creates a new object or function on every render (which is the default behavior), the shallow comparison will always see 'new' props and React.memo will have no effect. This is the most common mistake.

To make React.memo effective with non-primitive props, you need useMemo (for objects/arrays) and useCallback (for functions). useMemo returns a memoized value that only recomputes when its dependencies change. useCallback returns a memoized function reference that stays stable across renders. Together with React.memo, they form a complete memoization strategy.

However, memoization is not free. It adds memory overhead (storing previous values), CPU overhead (running comparisons), and code complexity. You should only memoize when you have measured a performance problem. The React Profiler or React DevTools Highlight Updates feature can show you which components re-render and how long they take. Memoize the expensive ones, not everything.

A good rule of thumb: use React.memo for components that (1) render often due to parent updates, (2) render the same output for the same props most of the time, and (3) are medium-to-large in complexity. Small components (a paragraph, a simple button) re-render so cheaply that memoization overhead exceeds the benefit.

codeExamples.title: React.memo preventing unnecessary re-renders
codeExamples.output: ExpensiveList renders once on mount. Clicking the button does NOT re-render it.
codeExamples.explanation: React.memo checks if items changed. Because items is memoized with useMemo and has no dependencies, it keeps the same reference. The shallow comparison passes, and ExpensiveList skips re-rendering.

codeExamples.title: useCallback for stable function references
codeExamples.output: SearchInput only re-renders when value changes, not when results update
codeExamples.explanation: Without useCallback, handleChange would be a new function on every render, causing React.memo to see different props every time. useCallback keeps the same reference.

codeExamples.title: Custom comparison function
codeExamples.explanation: The second argument to React.memo is a custom comparison function. Return true to skip re-render (props are equal), false to re-render. This gives fine-grained control beyond shallow comparison.

notes: React.memo only checks props. If the component uses context or internal state, it will still re-render when those change.
useMemo and useCallback are necessary companions to React.memo when passing objects or functions as props.
Profile before you memoize. Premature memoization adds complexity without measurable benefit.
commonPitfalls: Using React.memo but passing inline objects or arrow functions as props, which defeats memoization because the reference changes every render.
Memoizing every component in the app. The comparison cost and memory overhead can actually make performance worse for small, cheap-to-render components.
interviewTips: Explain the difference between React.memo (component-level), useMemo (value-level), and useCallback (function-level) - interviewers often ask you to distinguish them.
Mention that React.memo does a shallow comparison by default and explain why that matters for objects and functions.
Discuss when NOT to use memoization: small components, components that always receive new props, or when you haven't profiled to confirm a problem.
Be ready to explain that React's default behavior (re-render on parent update) is usually fine. The framework is optimized for this, and unnecessary memoization is a common code smell.

exercises.title: Shallow Comparison Implementation
exercises.description: Implement a shallowEqual function that compares two objects the way React.memo does. It should return true if all keys have the same values (using ===). Test it with both matching and non-matching objects.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Same values: true
Different values: false
Different keys: false
Same reference: true
Same array ref: true
Different array ref: false
hints: First check if the objects are the same reference (===). Then compare key counts. Then compare each value with ===.
Remember: === on arrays/objects compares by reference, not by contents. Two separate [1,2] arrays are NOT ===.

exercises.title: Memoized Component Simulation
exercises.description: Simulate React.memo behavior. Create a memoize wrapper that takes a render function and only calls it when props change (using shallow comparison). Log 'RENDER' when the function runs and 'SKIP' when memoization prevents it.
exercises.expectedOutput:
RENDER
Card: Alice, Age: 25
SKIP (memoized)
Card: Alice, Age: 25
RENDER
Card: Bob, Age: 30
SKIP (memoized)
Card: Bob, Age: 30
hints: Store the previous props and result. On each call, shallow-compare current props with previous. If equal, return the cached result.
Make sure to copy the props (spread into a new object) when storing them, so later mutations don't affect the comparison.

content.explanation:
Unnecessary re-renders are the most common performance problem in React applications. A re-render happens when a component's state changes, when its parent re-renders, or when a consumed context value changes. While React is fast at re-rendering, doing it thousands of times on every interaction creates noticeable lag, especially on mobile devices.

The first and most impactful technique is state colocation - moving state down to the component that actually needs it. If only a search input needs to track its value, that state should live in the search input component, not in a parent that also renders a heavy list. When state lives too high in the tree, every state change re-renders the entire subtree, including components that don't care about that state.

The children-as-props pattern is a powerful technique that is often overlooked. When a parent component has state that causes frequent re-renders, any JSX passed as the children prop is NOT re-created - it was created by the grandparent and passed down as a stable reference. This means wrapping expensive children in a stateful parent does not re-render the children on every state change.

Composition over wrapping is another pattern. Instead of one large component that manages multiple concerns, break it into smaller components where each manages only the state it needs. The expensive rendering work happens in components that don't have rapidly-changing state, so they stay stable.

Use the React DevTools Profiler to identify which components re-render and why. The 'Highlight updates' feature visually flashes components that re-render. The Profiler tab records render timings and shows you the commit with the longest duration. Always measure before optimizing - intuition about performance is often wrong.

codeExamples.title: State colocation - moving state down
codeExamples.output: In PageGood, typing in search does NOT re-render ExpensiveList
codeExamples.explanation: Moving the search state into SearchInput means only SearchInput re-renders on keystrokes. ExpensiveList is a sibling with no state dependency, so it stays stable.

codeExamples.title: Children as props pattern
codeExamples.output: ScrollTracker updates on scroll, but ExpensiveContent does not re-render
codeExamples.explanation: ExpensiveContent is created by Page (the parent of ScrollTracker) and passed as children. When ScrollTracker re-renders due to scrollY state change, children is the same reference - it was not recreated.

codeExamples.title: Identifying re-renders with a render counter
codeExamples.explanation: Child re-renders even though its props haven't changed, because React re-renders all children when a parent's state changes. Use React.memo on Child to prevent this.

notes: State colocation is the single most effective optimization - it reduces the scope of re-renders without any memoization overhead.
The children-as-props pattern works because React elements are just objects. If the same object reference is passed as children, React skips re-rendering it.
Use React DevTools Profiler to measure before optimizing. Focus on components that take more than 16ms to render (one frame at 60fps).
commonPitfalls: Keeping all state at the top of the component tree 'for convenience', causing the entire app to re-render on every state change.
Wrapping everything in React.memo instead of restructuring components. Memoization is a bandaid - composition and state colocation solve the root cause.

exercises.title: State Colocation Refactor
exercises.description: Simulate a bad component structure where state in a parent causes unnecessary re-renders of siblings. Then refactor using state colocation and show that the expensive sibling no longer re-renders.
exercises.expectedOutput:
=== Bad Structure ===
Input: H | Renders - Parent: 1, Input: 1, Expensive: 1
Input: He | Renders - Parent: 2, Input: 2, Expensive: 2
Input: Hel | Renders - Parent: 3, Input: 3, Expensive: 3
=== Bad Structure ===
Input: H | Renders - Parent: 1, Input: 1, Expensive: 1
Input: He | Renders - Parent: 2, Input: 2, Expensive: 2
Input: Hel | Renders - Parent: 3, Input: 3, Expensive: 3
=== Good Structure (Colocated) ===
Initial render - Page: 1, Expensive: 1
Input: H | Renders - Page: 1, SearchInput: 1, Expensive: 1
Input: He | Renders - Page: 1, SearchInput: 2, Expensive: 1
Input: Hel | Renders - Page: 1, SearchInput: 3, Expensive: 1
hints: In the bad structure, every state change increments ALL render counts. In the good structure, only SearchInput's count increases on keystrokes.
The key insight: in the good structure, Page and Expensive render only once (on mount) and never again when the search input changes.

exercises.title: Children as Props Pattern
exercises.description: Simulate the children-as-props optimization. Create a wrapper component that has frequently-changing state (like a timer). Show that children passed from the parent are not affected by the wrapper's state changes.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Children created once: ExpensiveContent (rendered 1 time(s))
Timer tick: 1 | Timer renders: 1 | Children: ExpensiveContent (rendered 1 time(s))
Timer tick: 2 | Timer renders: 2 | Children: ExpensiveContent (rendered 1 time(s))
Timer tick: 3 | Timer renders: 3 | Children: ExpensiveContent (rendered 1 time(s))
Children render count: 1
Timer render count: 3
hints: Create the children string ONCE before the timer loop. The children value stays the same across all timer ticks.
The timer re-renders 3 times but children are only created once - this is the key optimization of the children-as-props pattern.

content.explanation:
Code splitting is the practice of breaking your application's JavaScript bundle into smaller chunks that load on demand. Without code splitting, users download the entire app's code upfront - even for pages they never visit. This increases the initial load time and wastes bandwidth. Code splitting ensures users only download the code they need, when they need it.

React.lazy and dynamic import() are the primary tools for code splitting in React. React.lazy takes a function that calls import() and returns a component that loads on demand. The bundler (Vite, webpack) sees the dynamic import and automatically creates a separate chunk for that module and its dependencies. When the component first renders, the chunk downloads, and Suspense shows a fallback during the download.

Route-based splitting is the most impactful strategy because routes are natural loading boundaries. Users expect a brief loading moment when navigating between pages. By lazy-loading each route component, the initial bundle contains only the layout, navigation, and current page. Other pages load on demand. This can reduce the initial bundle by 50-80% in large applications.

Component-based splitting is useful for heavy features that not all users interact with: complex modals, rich text editors, chart libraries, or admin panels. Instead of including chart.js in the main bundle (it's 200KB+), lazy-load the chart component only when the user navigates to the analytics page.

Bundle analysis tools like rollup-plugin-visualizer (for Vite) or webpack-bundle-analyzer show you exactly what is in each chunk and how large it is. Run the analysis, identify the largest chunks, and split them. Common culprits are large libraries (moment.js, lodash, chart libraries) that should be dynamically imported or replaced with lighter alternatives.

codeExamples.title: Route-based code splitting
codeExamples.output: Initial bundle: Layout + routing. Each page loads on navigation.
codeExamples.explanation: Layout loads immediately. When the user navigates to /dashboard, only then does Dashboard.js download. Users who never visit /admin never download its code.

codeExamples.title: Component-based splitting for heavy features
codeExamples.output: Editor loads on mount. Charts only load when Preview is clicked.
codeExamples.explanation: The chart library (often 200KB+) is not in the initial bundle. It downloads only when the user clicks Preview, which many users may never do.

codeExamples.title: Prefetching on hover for instant navigation
codeExamples.output: Hovering triggers download; clicking shows the page instantly
codeExamples.explanation: Calling import() on hover starts the download. The browser caches the module. When React.lazy renders the component on navigation, the chunk is already available - no loading spinner.

notes: Route-based splitting gives the biggest bang for the buck - start there before splitting individual components.
React.lazy must be called at the module level, not inside a component. Defining it inside a component creates a new lazy component on every render.
Use a bundle analyzer to find the largest chunks and identify code-splitting opportunities.
commonPitfalls: Splitting too aggressively - every tiny component in its own chunk creates excessive HTTP requests and can actually slow things down due to request overhead.
Forgetting to wrap lazy components with Suspense, causing React to throw an error about missing Suspense boundaries.

exercises.title: Simulated Dynamic Import
exercises.description: Simulate the dynamic import and lazy loading pattern. Create a moduleLoader that registers modules and a lazyLoad function that loads them on demand. Track which modules have been loaded and log the loading process.
exercises.expectedOutput:
[Dashboard] Loading module...
[Dashboard] Loaded successfully
Result: Dashboard Component
[Dashboard] Already loaded (cached)
Result: Dashboard Component
[Settings] Loading module...
[Settings] Loaded successfully
Result: Settings Component
Loaded modules: 2/3
hints: Use two objects: registry (all available modules) and loaded (only modules that have been accessed). Check loaded first, then registry.
The factory function simulates the dynamic import - it's only called once, and the result is cached in the loaded object.

exercises.title: Bundle Size Impact Analysis
exercises.description: Simulate a bundle size analysis. Given a list of modules with their sizes, calculate the initial bundle size (only eagerly loaded modules) vs the total size. Show the savings from code splitting.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Initial bundle (eager):
  Framework: 45KB
  Router: 12KB
  Layout: 8KB
  Home: 15KB
Initial size: 80KB

Lazy-loaded chunks:
  Dashboard: 35KB
  Settings: 20KB
  Admin: 50KB
  ChartLib: 65KB
Lazy size: 170KB

Total: 250KB
Savings: 68% smaller initial load
hints: Filter modules by loading strategy. Sum sizes with reduce. Calculate percentage savings as (lazySize / totalSize * 100).
This demonstrates the real-world impact: users initially download 80KB instead of 250KB - a 68% reduction.

content.explanation:
List virtualization (also called windowing) is a technique for efficiently rendering large lists by only mounting the DOM nodes that are currently visible in the viewport. Instead of rendering 10,000 list items (creating 10,000 DOM nodes), virtualization renders only the ~20 items visible on screen plus a small buffer (overscan). As the user scrolls, items entering the viewport are mounted and items leaving are unmounted.

Without virtualization, a list of 10,000 items creates 10,000 DOM nodes. Each node takes memory, and the browser must lay out and paint all of them. Initial render can take several seconds, scrolling becomes janky, and memory usage spikes. Users on mobile devices with limited RAM may see the browser tab crash.

The react-window library (by Brian Vaughn, a React core team member) is the standard solution. It provides FixedSizeList for items with uniform height and VariableSizeList for items with different heights. You provide the total item count, item size, and a render function. react-window calculates which items are visible based on scroll position and only renders those.

The overscan property controls how many items are rendered outside the visible area. A small overscan (1-2 items) gives faster scroll performance but may show brief blank areas during fast scrolling. A larger overscan (5-10 items) provides smoother scrolling at the cost of rendering more items. The default of 1 works well for most cases.

Virtualization is most beneficial when you have hundreds or thousands of items. For lists under 100 items, the overhead of virtualization (measuring scroll position, calculating visible range) may exceed the cost of just rendering all items. Use it when you have large datasets and can confirm (via profiling) that rendering is the bottleneck.

codeExamples.title: Basic FixedSizeList with react-window
codeExamples.explanation: react-window renders only the visible items plus 5 overscan items above and below. The total DOM node count stays around 20 regardless of list length.

codeExamples.title: VariableSizeList for different item heights
codeExamples.explanation: VariableSizeList calls getItemSize for each index to determine the row height. estimatedItemSize helps with initial scroll bar sizing before all heights are measured.

codeExamples.title: Performance comparison: virtual vs non-virtual
codeExamples.explanation: The non-virtualized list creates 10,000 DOM nodes taking hundreds of milliseconds. The virtualized list creates ~20 nodes in under 5ms. The difference grows exponentially with list size.

notes: Virtualization is most impactful for lists with 500+ items. For smaller lists, plain rendering is usually fast enough.
The style prop in the row component is required - react-window uses absolute positioning to place each row, and the style contains the position and size.
react-window is lighter than react-virtualized. Use react-window for most cases; react-virtualized only if you need its extra features (multi-grid, table, collection).
commonPitfalls: Forgetting to apply the style prop to the row component - without it, all rows stack at position 0 and the list appears broken.
Using virtualization for short lists (under 100 items) where the overhead of windowing exceeds the cost of rendering all items.

exercises.title: Simulated Windowing Algorithm
exercises.description: Implement a basic windowing function. Given a total item count, viewport height, and item height, calculate which items should be visible. Return the start and end indices of the visible window.
exercises.expectedOutput:
--- At top (scrollTop: 0) ---
Start: 0, End: 7, Visible: 8
--- Scrolled down (scrollTop: 500) ---
Start: 10, End: 17, Visible: 8
--- Near bottom (scrollTop: 499500) ---
Start: 9990, End: 9997, Visible: 8
--- Rendered vs total ---
Rendering 8 of 10000 items (0.08%)
hints: startIndex = Math.floor(scrollTop / itemHeight) gives the first visible item. visibleCount = Math.ceil(viewportHeight / itemHeight) gives how many fit.
Clamp endIndex to totalItems - 1 to avoid going past the end of the list.

exercises.title: Virtual List Renderer
exercises.description: Build on the windowing calculator to create a virtual list renderer. Given a list of items, render only the visible ones based on scroll position. Include overscan (extra items above and below the visible area).
exercises.expectedOutput:
=== Top of list ===
Scroll: 0px | Window: [0-5] | Rendered: 6/1000
  Item 1
  Item 2
  Item 3
  Item 4
  (buffer) Item 5
  (buffer) Item 6
=== Scrolled to middle ===
Scroll: 25000px | Window: [498-505] | Rendered: 8/1000
  (buffer) Item 499
  (buffer) Item 500
  Item 501
  Item 502
  Item 503
  Item 504
  (buffer) Item 505
  (buffer) Item 506
hints: Calculate the raw visible range first, then expand it by overscan in both directions. Clamp to [0, items.length - 1].
Mark items outside the raw visible range but inside the overscan range as buffer items to show the difference.
